items.myth: UMAP always preserves exact global distances from high dimensions.
items.reality: UMAP balances local and global fuzzy structure but cannot isometrically embed high-D data in 2D; far-apart regions are approximated, not copied exactly.
items.example: Inter-cluster distances in a UMAP plot can reorder when n_neighbors changes even though local neighborhoods stay similar.

items.myth: UMAP and PCA solve the same problem—pick whichever looks prettier.
items.reality: PCA is a linear variance-preserving projection with explained-variance diagnostics; UMAP is a non-linear neighborhood embedder for exploration. Use PCA as baseline, UMAP when linear views fail.
items.example: Concentric rings separate in UMAP but overlap on PC1–PC2 because variance follows radius, not class angle.

items.myth: min_dist changes the k-NN graph in high dimensions.
items.reality: n_neighbors and metric define the high-dimensional graph; min_dist only shapes how tightly points pack during low-dimensional optimization.
items.example: Same n_neighbors=30 with min_dist=0.0 vs 0.8 yields different cluster tightness but identical neighbor sets before embedding.

items.myth: UMAP is a drop-in replacement for t-SNE with identical behavior.
items.reality: Both visualize neighborhoods but optimize different objectives; t-SNE often creates tighter local islands while UMAP frequently shows more continuous global layout—compare both on your data.
items.example: A single-cell dataset may show disconnected islands in t-SNE and a connected trajectory in UMAP with moderate n_neighbors.
